fmt/585.name: MPEG-2 Transport Stream
fmt/585.description: MPEG-2 is a lossy compression standard (an improvement and expansion of the MPEG-1 standard) designed for the generic coding of video and audio and intended to cover a wide scope of application, including: video production, broadcast and editing. Streams are at potentially higher bitrates than MPEG-1 but more efficient compression making it suited to High Definition video. MPEG-2 Video supports both 'progressive' and 'interlaced' frame video. An MPEG-2 'Transport' stream file (TS) file (*.ts, *.m2ts) is designed for low-latency, real-time transmission and is used in broadcasting. It can contain multiple programmes, not necessarily intended for synchronised playback. TS incorporates features that can allow flexible access to and playback of stream content.
fmt/585.created_by: National Library of Wales / National Library of Wales

fmt/83.name: Drawing Interchange File Format (Binary)
fmt/83.description: Binary DXF format is the binary encoded variant of the Drawing Interchange File Format, an exchange format for vector graphics developed by AutoDesk. They are probably the widely-used formats for exchanging vector data, and have become a de-facto industry standard. The format is owned by AutoDesk and typically changes with each release of their AutoCAD family of products. DXF files contain a tagged representation of the vector data. The structure comprises a Header section, followed by sections containing data on Classes, Tables, Blocks, Entities and, optionally, thumbnail images. Each section contains a series of data elements. each preceded by a group code tag, which indicates the type of data element. Binary DXF was first developed in AutoCAD Release 10. It should not be confused with the DXB format - this is an entirely separate AutoDesk format for binary encoding vector graphics. Binary DXF Release 14 is the version of the format associated with Release 14 of the AutoCAD software.
fmt/83.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.note: File header, header section with $ACADVER group code (AC1014), EOF marker

fmt/1474.name: TEI P4 XML - Single Text File
fmt/1474.description:
The Text Encoding Initiative Guidelines provide a methodology for encoding textual content for a wide variety of academic and publishing purposes and repurposes. P4 is serialised as XML. 

Conceptually, TEI is a sibling of HTML which focuses on textual semantics rather than display. 

The ‘ODD’ file extension is used when TEI is being used as a literate programming language for XML schemas, modern TEI guidelines are written in this form of the language, HTML, RNG, RNC and DTD files are then generated from the TEI. 

Note that TEI permits customisations and some may mean that the file no longer matches the attached signatures. This is especially the case where TEI is fragmented, embedded within other XML or when other XML namespaces are used.

Information on how to convert TEI P4 to TEI P5 can be found at https://www.tei-c.org/Vault/P4/migrate.html and a script at https://www.tei-c.org/Vault/P4/p4top5.xsl Such automated conversion is not guaranteed to be lossless, particularly when the TEI has been customised. 

P4 differs from the proceeding P3 version by being in XML rather than in SGML
fmt/1474.created_by: Victoria University of Wellington / Victoria University of Wellington

sequences.name: TEI P4 XML - Single Text
sequences.note: BOF:'<?xml version=("OR')1.0("OR')' followed by a gap between 0-512 bytes followed by 'TEI.2'

fmt/1582.name: Vim SWAP File
fmt/1582.description:
Vim SWAP file is a temporary file created by the open source Vim text editor, when editing a file. The file's name typically begins with ".", and ends with ".swp". The file is normally deleted when editing is complete, but may be left behind in the event of a crash or other abnormal termination. Its main purpose is to periodically record unsaved changes made to the file. It also serves as a lock file. The file format is undocumented, and not necessarily portable.

Vim is free and open-source, screen-based text editor program for Unix, released to the public in 1991. Vim was designed for use both from a command-line interface and as a standalone application in a graphical user interface.
fmt/1582.created_by: The National Archives and Records Administration / The National Archives and Records Administration

sequences.name: Vim SWAP file
sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 0; Magic Bytes: 'b0VIM'

fmt/682.name: Thumbs DB file
fmt/682.description: A thumbs.db file is generated automatically within Microsoft Windows environments (although the automatic creation behaviour can be turned off). It is generally a hidden system file and its purpose is to hold thumbnail images of any images stored within a given folder. This format is based on the OLE2 Compound Document Format and can be identified in DROID 6 and later via a container signature. Structurally it holds a Catalog file that indexes the thumbnails, and a file per image that contains the image data in a JPEG format with additional data preceeding the image data. At this time the signature is the result of reverse engineering work conducted by Ross Spencer, Richard Lehane and others. The Catalog file consists of a 4 byte opening sequence that appears to act as an identifier and database version number, followed by two bytes indicating the number of images within the thumbs.db file, followed by information relating to the resolution of the images. This signature is likely to be improved with further research and a better understanding of the format structure. This particular format is known to be produced in Windows XP environments, but may also apply to other Windows operating systems. Later thumbs.db files hold a different structure which cannot yet be suitably represented within a DROID container signature.
fmt/682.created_by: National Library of New Zealand / National Library of New Zealand

fmt/791.name: Material Exchange Format
fmt/791.description:
MXF is a media container format which supports a number of different streams of coded "essence", encoded with any of a variety of codecs, together with a metadata wrapper which describes the material contained within the MXF file. 

MXF allows for different degrees of complexity within the wrapper, which are standardised and defined as Operational Patterns, and this may be a consideration for an institution considering preserving the format, hence the separation of these Operational Patterns into separate PUIDs.

Operational Pattern “OP-Atom” is intended to satisfy the demands of applications where there is a requirement for exchange of material and associated metadata together in a single file with an open and standardized format, yet also a demand for a simple, predictable, layout with minimum scope for variation. OP-Atom is specifically intended for applications where each Essence Track is held separately.
fmt/791.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.name: MXF OP-ATOM
sequences.note: Header ..+4.......... then Operational Pattern Label ..+4.........

fmt/1673.name: ZBrush MatCap
fmt/1673.description: ZBrush Material format is used with 3-D materials by the ZBrush software. It is used with the MatCap (Material Capture) tool that uses calibration markers to capture information from photographs and other images. Each of these markers samples a single point on the image and defines the colour, texture and lightining of the surface to build up a material map that simulates the lighting and material properties from the photograph. The HDRP replaces all of the materials and lighting in the scene with what is in the MatCap.
fmt/1673.created_by: The National Archives and Records Administration / The National Archives and Records Administration

fmt/52.name: Rich Text Format
fmt/52.description: Rich Text Format (RTF) is a format developed by Microsoft to encode formatted text and graphics for use within applications or for data and formatting transfer between applications. It is closely associated with Microsoft Word, and new versions are developed with each release of Word. RTF files are encoded in plain text, typically ASCII, and consists of a series of control words, control symbols and groups. An RTF file comprises a Header section, containing various header tables, followed by a Document section, containing the document content and formatting. RTF version 1.7 is the version of the format associated with Microsoft Word 2002 (XP).
fmt/52.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/66.name: Microsoft Access Database File
x-fmt/66.description: Microsoft Access Database File, formerly known as Jet MDB Database format, is propriatory file format developed and used by Microsoft as a native format for its Microsoft Access (formerly Microsoft Jet Database Engine) desktop database management system. First released in 1992, starting with version 1.0 the earlier versions of the database used the Jet Database Engine, with version 1.0 having a maximum size of 128 megabytes, then increasing to 1 gigabyte with versions 1.1 and 2.0. This entry records the version 2.0 of MDB file format, for a full list of versions please follow the links provided in the Notes section.

x-fmt/239.name: Microsoft Access Database File
x-fmt/239.description: Microsoft Access Database File, formerly known as Jet MDB Database format, is proprietary file format developed and used by Microsoft as a native format for its Microsoft Access (formerly Microsoft Jet Database Engine) desktop database management system. First released in 1992, starting with version 1.0 the earlier versions of the database used the Jet Database Engine, with version Microsoft Access 97 utilizing the Jet 3.5 database engine. The page size of the Jet 3 database engine is 2048 bytes. The .mde extension refers to protected Access database file with compiled VBA and macros. This entry records the version 97 of MDB file format, for a full list of versions please follow the links provided in the Notes section.

x-fmt/142.name: Computer Graphics Metafile ASCII
x-fmt/142.description:
Computer Graphics Metafile (CGM) is a standardized platform-independent format used for the interchange of raster and vector data. CGM was developed under the auspices of the International Standards Organization (ISO) and the American Standards National Institute (ANSI). It is also used in conjunction with a variety of input and output devices. Although CGM incorporates extensions designed to support raster data storage, files in CGM format are used primarily to store vector information. CGM files typically contain either bitmap or vector data, but rarely both. It uses Clear Text Encoding.
File format specifications available at http://standards.iso.org/ittf/PubliclyAvailableStandards/c044114_ISO_IEC_8632-1_1999_Cor_1_2006%28E%29.pdf
x-fmt/142.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.name: Computer Graphics Metafile (Clear Text) Version 1 No.1
sequences.note: Element name begmf (Begin Metafile) with any letter in the element name in upper or lower case, followed by any number of bytes, followed by version (MFVERSION{1}1 or mfversion{1}1, or any upper/lower case combination). Trailer endmf{0-1}; (with any letter in the element name in upper or lower case) that can be followed by a comment

x-fmt/218.name: ESRI Arc/Info Binary Grid
x-fmt/218.description: An Arc/Info binary grid is a proprietary raster GIS file format developed by ESRI. The grid defines geographic space as an array of equally sized square grid points arranged in rows and columns. Each grid point stores a numeric value that represents a geographic attribute such as elevation or surface slope. Each grid cell is referenced by its x,y coordinate.

x-fmt/271.name: dBASE Database
x-fmt/271.description: dBase is a database management systems for microcomputers.The dBase system includes the core database engine, a query system, a forms engine, and a programming language that ties all of these components together. dBase's underlying file format, the .dbf file, is widely used in applications needing a simple format to store structured data.

x-fmt/226.name: ESRI Arc/Info Export File
x-fmt/226.description:
ESRI ArcInfo Export File Format (also known as ArcInfo Interchange File Format) is a vector file format which is produced by ESRI and pre-dates the shapefile. It is as an export file used to enable coverage, grid or TIN and an associated INFO table to be transferred between different machines, which are not connected by any type of file sharing network. A volume has the extension .E00 through .E99. 
A non-authoritative version of the specification can be found at: http://avce00.maptools.org/docs/v7_e00_cover.html
x-fmt/226.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.name: ESRI Arc/Info Export File
sequences.note: Header line beginning with EXP, followed by a constant of 0 or 1. Footer sequence ASCII: ..EOI..EOS..

x-fmt/159.name: GEM Image
x-fmt/159.description:
The GEM raster format (also known as GEM VDI Bit Image; GEM Bit Image; GEM Image; GEM Bitmap; or IMG) is an image format native to the Graphical Environment Manager (GEM) operating system and developed at Digital Research. The format was used by GEM Paint and Ventura Publisher, and by a variety of Atari ST software.

GEM raster images may be color, gray scale, or black and white and are always read and written in the big-endian format. The format of bi-level images is well standardized, but the specification is ambiguous as to the format of color images. As a result, other color formats of GEM raster image were later developed, some of which have their own names, such as XIMG (Extended GEM Bit Image), TIMG (TrueColor IMG), STTT, or Hyperpaint.
x-fmt/159.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/158.name: Initial Graphics Exchange Specification (IGES)
x-fmt/158.description: Initial Graphics Exchange Specification, or IGES, establishes information structures for the digital representation and exchange of product definition data. It supports exchanging this data among Computer-Aided Design and Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAD and CAM, respectively) systems.  IGES defines file structure and language formats to represent geometric, topographical, and non-geometric product definition data.  These formats are independent of the modeling method used, and they support data exchange using physical media or electronic communication protocols.
x-fmt/158.created_by: The National Archives and Records Administration / The National Archives and Records Administration

x-fmt/233.name: Paint Shop Pro Image
x-fmt/233.description: The Paint Shop Pro (PSP) file format is an extremely rich graphics file format that is capable of describing a multi-layered image document in minute detail. The format facilitates the retention of a great deal of information, including layer bitmaps, layer masks, layer grouping information, a sample thumbnail, image dimension, image resolution, creator name, copyright owner, image description, masks, selections, alpha channels, etc. The PSP format supports storing an image document in an uncompressed format or, to reduce the size of the file, in either Run Length Encoding (RLE) or LZ77 compressed format. As each of these is a lossless storage method, the PSP format always maintains the original quality of stored image documents. Finally, the PSP format supports storing image bitmaps at a variety of bit depths, including 24 bit colour (16.7 million colours), 8 bit colour (256 colour), 8 bit greyscale (256 greys), 4 bit colour (16 colours) and 1 bit colour (2 colours).
x-fmt/233.created_by: The National Archives and Records Administration / The National Archives and Records Administration

sequences.name: Paint Shop Pro Image 3
sequences.note: Header 32 byte PSP signature (ASCII: Paint Shop Pro Image File); followed by 2 byte little-endian major version

x-fmt/234.name: Paint Shop Pro Image
x-fmt/234.description: The Paint Shop Pro (PSP) file format is an extremely rich graphics file format that is capable of describing a multi-layered image document in minute detail. The format facilitates the retention of a great deal of information, including layer bitmaps, layer masks, layer vector data, layer adjustment data, layer grouping information, multiple sample thumbnails, multiple sample composite images, image dimension, image resolution, creator name, copyright owner, image description, masks, selections, alpha channels, etc. The PSP format supports storing image bitmaps in an uncompressed format or, to reduce the size of the file, in either Run Length Encoding (RLE) or LZ77 compressed format. As each of these is a lossless storage method, the PSP format always maintains the original quality of stored image documents. Finally, the PSP format supports storing image bitmaps at a variety of bit depths, including 24-bit colour (16.7 million colours), 8-bit colour (256 colour), 8-bit greyscale (256 greys), 4-bit colour (16 colours), and 1-bit colour (2 colours).
x-fmt/234.created_by: The National Archives and Records Administration / The National Archives and Records Administration

sequences.name: Paint Shop Pro Image 5
sequences.note: Header 32 byte PSP signature (ASCII: Paint Shop Pro Image File); followed by 2 byte little-endian major version

x-fmt/274.name: Microsoft Word for MS-DOS Document
x-fmt/274.description: Microsoft Word for MS-DOS Document is a proprietary binary format of Microsoft Word, word processing application, for MS-DOS first released in 1983. The first version was released on IBM PC DOS, developed by Microsoft, which Microsoft then re-branded as MS-DOS. The version was developed by Charles Simonyi and Richard Brodie firstly for Xenix in February 1983 (titled as "Multi-Tool Word") and in October 1983 for MS-DOS operating systems. The application featured graphics video mode, mouse and printer support (printer support was added in version 2) in WYSIWYG (What You See Is What You Get) interface and could run in text mode or graphics mode, with bold, italics, and underline features only available in the text mode. The following four versions were not much dissimilar, including only minor improvements, with the .doc format remained with the same therefore, they are all part of this entry. Please see the full list of versions using the links provided in the Notes section as well as, the manual for MS-DOS versions 1x which can be downloaded from WinWorld.
x-fmt/274.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/275.name: Microsoft Word for MS-DOS Document
x-fmt/275.description: Microsoft Word for MS-DOS Document is a proprietary binary format of Microsoft Word, word processing application, for MS-DOS first released in 1983. The first version was released on IBM PC DOS, developed by Microsoft, which Microsoft then re-branded as MS-DOS. The version was developed by Charles Simonyi and Richard Brodie firstly for Xenix in February 1983 (titled as "Multi-Tool Word") and in October 1983 for MS-DOS operating systems. The application featured graphics video mode, mouse and printer support (printer support was added in version 2) in WYSIWYG (What You See Is What You Get) interface and could run in text mode or graphics mode, with bold, italics, and underline features only available in the text mode. The version 5 of the format was released in 1989, with the release of Word for MS-MOS, which included added support for bookmarks, cross-references and conditions and loops in macros, remaining backwards compatible with Word 3.0 macros. Please see the full list of versions using the links provided in the Notes section.
x-fmt/275.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.name: OS/2 Bitmap 2.0
sequences.note:
2-byte file type identifier ASCII “BM” at beginning of a 14-byte file header; offset 14 little-endian long integer size of bitmap header (16, 64); offset 26 2-byte little-endian number of bit planes (always 1). 
The 2 byte file type identifier BM indicates that the BMP was created by OS/2 or Windows. Windows 3.x, Windows NT, and OS/2 2.x BMP files only vary in the number of fields in the bitmap header and in the interpretation of the compression field. Bitmap headerSize is 12 for Windows 2.x BMP and OS/2 1.x BMP, 40 for Windows 3.x and Windows NT BMP, 12 to 64 for OS/2 2.x BMP, and 108 for Windows 4.x BMP. Only OS/2 2.x bitmaps have Bitmap header size of 16 and 64. If Bitmap header size is 40, only OS/2 Bitmaps can use 24-bit RLE compression on 24-bit pixel bitmaps and only oS/2 bitmaps can use Huffman 1D compression on 1-bit per pixel (monochrome) bitmaps.

x-fmt/276.name: Microsoft Word for MS-DOS Document
x-fmt/276.description: Microsoft Word for MS-DOS Document is a proprietary binary format of Microsoft Word, word processing application, for MS-DOS first released in 1983. The first version was released on IBM PC DOS, developed by Microsoft, which Microsoft then re-branded as MS-DOS. The version was developed by Charles Simonyi and Richard Brodie firstly for Xenix in February 1983 (titled as "Multi-Tool Word") and in October 1983 for MS-DOS operating systems. The application featured graphics video mode, mouse and printer support (printer support was added in version 2) in WYSIWYG (What You See Is What You Get) interface and could run in text mode or graphics mode, with bold, italics, and underline features only available in the text mode. x-fmt/297.name: Paint Shop Pro Image
x-fmt/297.description: The Paint Shop Pro (PSP) file format is an extremely rich graphics file format that is capable of describing a multi-layered image document in minute detail. The format facilitates the retention of a great deal of information, including layer bitmaps, layer masks, layer vector data, layer adjustment data, layer grouping information, multiple sample thumbnails, multiple sample composite images, image dimension, image resolution, creator name, copyright owner, image description, EXIF information, masks, selections, alpha channels, etc. The PSP format supports storing image bitmaps in an uncompressed format or, to reduce the size of the file, in either Run Length Encoding (RLE) or LZ77 compressed format. As each of these is a lossless storage method, the PSP format always maintains the original quality of stored image documents. Finally, the PSP format supports storing image bitmaps at a variety of bit depths, including 24-bit colour (16.7 million colours), 8-bit colour (256 colour), 8-bit greyscale (256 greys), 4-bit colour (16 colours), and 1-bit colour (2 colours).
x-fmt/297.created_by: The National Archives and Records Administration / The National Archives and Records Administration

x-fmt/108.name: STL (Standard Tessellation Language) ASCII
x-fmt/108.description: STL is a 3D modelling file format created by 3D Systems. An STL file is a triangular representation of a 3-dimensional surface geometry. The surface is tessellated or broken down logically into a series of small triangles (facets). Each facet is described by a perpendicular direction and three points representing the vertices (corners) of the triangle. These data are used by a slicing algorithm to determine the cross sections of the 3-dimensional shape to be built by the fabricator.
x-fmt/108.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.name: STL
sequences.note: <optional blank>solid + … + “facet normal” + … + “outer loop” + … + vertex

x-fmt/237.name: WordStar for MS-DOS Document
x-fmt/237.description: The WordStar format is the default proprietary plain text format for the word processing application of the same name. It was created in 1978 by MicroPro, originally for the CP/M operating system but later was used on other operating systems such as PC/MS-DOS. It was the first word processor to render the doument on screen as it would appear when printed. WordStar files often have the extension ws or ws follwed by their version number but it is also common for them to have no extension.
x-fmt/237.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/260.name: WordStar for MS-DOS Document
x-fmt/260.description:
The WordStar format is the default proprietary plain text format for the word processing application of the same name. It was created in 1978 by MicroPro, originally for the CP/M operating system but later was used on other operating systems such as PC/MS-DOS. It was the first word processor to render the doument on screen as it would appear when printed. WordStar files often have the extension ws or ws follwed by their version number but it is also common for them to have no extension. 

WS4 files currently cannot by identified though a PRONOM identification signature. 

On the surface it's a plain text file, however the format 'shifts' the last byte of each word. Effectively it is 'flipping' the first bit of the ASCII character from 0 to 1. so a lower case 'r' (hex value 0x72) becomes 'ò' (hex value 0xF2); lower case 'd' (hex 0x64) becomes 'ä' (hex 0xE4) and so on. 

It should be possible to write a simple decoder application for WS4 files, however note that the conjecture above is based on observation of a limited pool of files, and there may be additional subtleties yet to be encountered and understood. Please contact the PRONOM team if you have any further information about this format.
x-fmt/260.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/110.name: Fixed Width Values Text File
x-fmt/110.description: In a fixed-width text file, data is contained in columns that are of a fixed width (meaning that each column contains a certain number of character positions). Data that does not fill the width of the column is padded with filler characters, which are usually spaces or zeroes. Sometimes, your fixed-width text file uses special characters to visually delineate one column from the next.
x-fmt/110.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/152.name: Digital Video
x-fmt/152.description: The Digital Video file format was developed as a videocassette format in the 1990s by a consortium of consumer video companies and is described in the IEC 61834 series of standards. DV video content utilizes Discrete Cosine Transform compression, but allows for higher quality than formats that use similar compression schemes. DV files can be "raw" with the .dv file extension or wrapped into video container formats.
x-fmt/152.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/213.name: Quicken Data File
x-fmt/213.description: A .QDF file is a  Quicken Data file. The .qdf file extension is used for a data file format developed by Intuit, Inc. for their personal finance and money management software Quicken. This software provides users with tools for calculating and managing their financial records and tax details. These .qdf files are also called Quicken Data Files, and they contain a user's financial and accounting data. Earlier Quicken versions used file extensions QDB and QDT for this data.
x-fmt/213.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

x-fmt/249.name: Microsoft Outlook Personal Folders (Unicode)
x-fmt/249.description:
The Personal Folders File or PST is an open proprietary data file format used to store local copies of messages, calendar events, and other items within Microsoft software including Microsoft Office Outlook. PST files are used to store archived items and to maintain offline availability of the items. 

PST is a stand-alone, self-contained, structured binary file format that does not require any external dependencies. Each PST file represents a message store that contains an arbitrary hierarchy of Folder objects, which contains Message objects, which can contain Attachment objects. Information about Folder objects, Message objects, and Attachment objects are stored in properties, which collectively contain all of the information about the particular item.

PST_Unicode is the default format used by Office Outlook versions starting with Outlook 2003 and includes Outlook 2007, Outlook 2010 and Outlook 2013. It employs the Unicode character set.

The file size constraints for PST_Unicode are significantly larger than the PST_ANSI overall size limit of 2 gigabytes (GB). PST_Unicode can support file sizes up to 20 GB in Outlook 2003 and Outlook 2007 and file sizes up to 50 GB for Outlook 2010 and Outlook 2013. According to Microsoft, these file size limits can be extended but would negatively impact performance.
x-fmt/249.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.name: Personal Storage Table *.pst (Unicode)
sequences.note: Byte analysis completed in http://downloads.sourceforge.net/libpff/Personal_Foler_File_Format.pdf (Personal Folder File (PFF) File Format Specification) and sequence confirmed by research completed in http://www.five-ten-sg.com/libpst/rn01re06.html. PST file header, 24 bytes in total.

x-fmt/248.name: Microsoft Outlook Personal Folders (ANSI)
x-fmt/248.description:
The Personal Folders File or PST is an open proprietary data file format used to store local copies of messages, calendar events, and other items within Microsoft software including Microsoft Office Outlook. PST files are used to store archived items and to maintain offline availability of the items. 

PST is a stand-alone, self-contained, structured binary file format that does not require any external dependencies. Each PST file represents a message store that contains an arbitrary hierarchy of Folder objects, which contains Message objects, which can contain Attachment objects. Information about Folder objects, Message objects, and Attachment objects are stored in properties, which collectively contain all of the information about the particular item.

Now considered a legacy format and replaced by PST_Unicode, PST_ANSI was used by Microsoft Outlook 97-2002. It employs the American National Standards Institute (ANSI) character set and has an overall size limit of 2 gigabytes (GB). PST_ANSI uses 32-bit values to represent block IDs (BIDs) and byte index (IB).
x-fmt/248.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

sequences.name: Personal Storage Table *.pst (ANSI)
sequences.note: Byte analysis completed in http://downloads.sourceforge.net/libpff/Personal_Foler_File_Format.pdf (Personal Folder File (PFF) File Format Specification) and sequence confirmed by research completed in http://www.five-ten-sg.com/libpst/rn01re06.html. PST file header, 24 bytes in total.

x-fmt/316.name: Dr Halo Bitmap
x-fmt/316.description: The Dr. Halo Bitmap file format, created by Media Cybernetics in 1984, was designed to facilitate image transfer between MS-DOS applications, making it one of the early device-independent bitmap formats. It is closely associated with the HALO Image File Format Library and software like the Dr. Halo raster graphics editor. The format primarily includes a .CUT file for bitmap image data and a .PAL file for color palette information. While initially popular in MS-DOS environments for basic graphic interchange, the Dr. Halo format was eventually superseded by more versatile formats as personal computing advanced.
x-fmt/316.created_by: Digital Preservation Department / The National Archives

fmt/2051.name: Cineon
fmt/2051.description: Cineon is an image format created by Kodak designed to represent scanned film images. Individual Cineon files are raster images, however the typical use is for a sequence of Cineon images to represent frame-by-frame digital motion picture video. Digital Moving Picture Exchange Bitmap (DPX) is directly descended from Cineon.

sequences.name: Cineon
sequences.note: Absolute from BOF, magic bytes: 0x802A5FD7

fmt/2044.name: OpenDocument Text
fmt/2044.description: OpenDocument (ODF) format is a generic format for office documents, such as text, spreadsheets, presentations, drawings, and databases. It was developed by OASIS, based on the original OpenOffice document format. The format is an open standard, and is supported by a variety of office software packages. ODF is an XML format, which may comprise a single XML document, or a collection of subdocuments within a package. The package format is the commonly used form, and comprises a standard ZIP package containing the XML content and any associated binary data, together with a manifest which lists the package content. An ODF text document contains word-processed text. The content is contained in a 'content' xml subdocument. Additional subdocuments contain style information, document metadata, and application-specific settings.

fmt/2050.name: PDF/UA Portable Document Format
fmt/2050.description: Standardized by ISO 14289-1:2014, PDF/UA-1 is a constrained subset of PDF 1.7 (ISO 32000-1) focused on maximising accessibility and support for assistive technologies. Note that files may conform to both PDF/UA and PDF/A, which will result in multiple identification outcomes from DROID. Neither format is the more specific PDF subset, so it is inappropriate to prioritise identification of one over the other.

sequences.name: PDF/UA-1
sequences.note: BOF: ‘%PDF-1.7’ then later in the document ‘<pdfuaid:part>1</pdfuaid:part>’

fmt/2056.name: Immersive Audio Model Format
fmt/2056.description: IAMF is a spatial audio format intended as a royalty-free alternative to Dolby Atmos, developed by the Alliance for Open Media. This entry covers a standalone IAMF file that is not contained by ISO-BMFF (MP4) or other audio container format.

sequences.name: Immersive Audio Model Format
sequences.note: Aboslute from BOF, magic bytes: obu_type=OBU_IA_Sequence_Header (0xF8 or 0xFC), size: 6 bytes (0x06), ia_code=’iamf’ (Noted here: https://aomediacodec.github.io/iamf/v1.0.0-errata.html#ia_code)

fmt/2057.name: A2R Disk Image File
fmt/2057.description: The A2R disk image format provides a detailed, low-level capture of the contents of Apple II and early Macintosh disks. Due to its comprehensive nature, A2R files are significantly larger than other formats like the WOZ disk image format, which aim to replicate only the data originally written to the disk. Instead, A2R captures the raw magnetic patterns of the disk, making it easier to work with old, barely readable disks, although it requires further processing to extract the original data.
fmt/2057.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: A2R Disk Image File v.2 (little endian)
sequences.note: BOF, offset zero, magic bytes: A2R2ÿ

fmt/2055.name: Apple Mail EMLX Format
fmt/2055.description: The EMLX file format, introduced by Apple for MacOS in 2005 with Mac OS X Tiger (10.4) in Mail version 2, is used to store email files in Apple Mail. It is a proprietary variation of the EML format, with added byte count and property list (plist) features to record email metadata. Each email is stored as an individual EMLX file, with the byte count at the beginning indicating the email’s size.
fmt/2055.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

fmt/2049.name: ArcGIS Pro Layer File
fmt/2049.description: ArcGIS Pro Layer Files are metadata storage files used in ArcGIS Pro which store all settings for layer file properties. As they are proprietary, they are directly readable only by ArcGIS software. Lyrx files are in a JSON-based text format.

fmt/2054.name: JSON Lines Text Format
fmt/2054.description:
The JSON Lines text format, also called newline-delimited JSON. JSON Lines is used to store structured data that may be processed one record at a time, and is also a flexible format for passing messages between cooperating processes. It works well with unix-style text processing tools, shell pipelines and log files.
There are three requirements to JSONL:
UTF-8 Encoding
Each Line is a Valid JSON Value
Line Separator is '\n'

fmt/2061.name: MOOF Disk Image File
fmt/2061.description: A MOOF file utilizes a chunk-based binary format, ensuring future-proof compatibility with older software which may not recognize newer data chunks. MOOF serves as the Macintosh equivalent of the WOZ disk image format for floppy disks. The name "MOOF" references Dogcow, a character from Macintosh folklore: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dogcow
fmt/2061.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: MOOF Disk Image v.1 (little endian)
sequences.note: Absolute from BOF, magic bytes: MOOFÿ

fmt/2060.name: WOZ Disk Image File
fmt/2060.description: WOZ is a disk image format for Apple II, introduced in 2018. It aims to faithfully reproduce the data layout of a disk, even those with proprietary copy-protection schemes. This format is connected to the Applesauce project.
fmt/2060.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: WOZ Disk Image File v.2 (little endian)
sequences.note: Absolute from BOF, magic bytes: WOZ2ÿ

fmt/2065.name: TOML
fmt/2065.description: TOML (Tom’s Obvious Minimal Language) was created by Tom Preston-Werner, in the github repository tom-lang. TOML is a minimal configuration file format that is designed with clear semantics so as to be easily human-readable. TOML is designed to map unambiguously to a hash table (synonymous with a dictionary or map) and so can be parsed and understood by a number of programming languages. Data structures represented by TOML can often be mapped into other languages as required such as JSON or YAML.

fmt/2063.name: DaVinci Resolve Timeline File
fmt/2063.description: Files produced by the DaVinci Resolve software, which is used for video editing, colour correction and grading, visual effects and post production audio. Since version 9 the files have had the extension .drt. Timeline files are project files which contain only the timeline and its associated media, rather than the entire project. The software was developed by da Vinci Systems in 2004 and then purchased by BlackMagic Design in 2009.
fmt/2063.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

fmt/2062.name: AV1 Image File Format
fmt/2062.description: An open image format based on the AV1 codec in HEIF.
fmt/2062.created_by: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints / The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints

sequences.name: AV1 Image File Format
sequences.note: Absolute from BOF, offset 4, maximum Offset 4: ftypavif

fmt/2064.name: DaVinci Resolve Project File
fmt/2064.description: Files produced by the DaVinci Resolve software which is used for video editing, colour correction and grading, visual effects and post production audio. Since version 9 the files have had the extension .drp, but before that they often used .resolve.zip. The files do not contain the actual media, only project details. They are ZIP containers and the software was developed by da Vinci Systems in 2004 and then purchased by BlackMagic Design in 2009.
fmt/2064.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

fmt/2066.name: Rust Source File
fmt/2066.description: .rs files are typically source code files for the rust programming language.

fmt/2067.name: XYZ Coordinate Data
fmt/2067.description: Files with the .xyz extension are most commonly ASCII (plain text) files containing spatial data expressed using the Cartesian coordinate system (x, y, and z). As they are usually simple lists of coordinates it is not possible to create a viable and consistently accurate PRONOM identification signature for these files at this time. There may be formats that use the .xyz extension that can be reliably identified and these should get their own PRONOM entries where appropriate.

fmt/2068.name: Macintosh File System
fmt/2068.description: Macintosh File System is the original Macintosh file system created for use on single sided 400K floppy disks and MacOS system 6 and before. It was released in January 1984 and effectively discontinued in September 1985 with the introduction of Hierarchical File System (fmt/1105).
fmt/2068.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: Macintosh File System
sequences.note: BOF< offset 1024, magic bytes: Ò×{12}..{6}[!&.].

fmt/2078.name: Common Ground Digital Paper
fmt/2078.description: Common Ground  Digital Paper files are generated from Common Ground from No Hands Software, which allows any Windows or Mac program to create a document that can be viewed or printed by users even if they don't have the program that was used to create the document in the first instance. Common Grounds was used to create portable, self-contained documentation applications called "Digital Paper"
fmt/2078.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: Common Ground Digital Paper 3
sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 8; Magic Bytes: 'DPL2'

fmt/2069.name: Draco
fmt/2069.description: Draco is a library for compressing and decompressing 3D geometric meshes and point clouds. It is intended to provide efficient storage and transmission of 3D graphics.

sequences.name: Draco v.2
sequences.note: BOF; Offset:0; Magic Bytes: 'DRACO' followed by 0x02

fmt/2076.name: ACE
fmt/2076.description: ACE is a compressed archive format, originally developed by Marcel Lemke and later by e-merge GmbH. Some relevant software includes Ace (for DOS, OS/2 and Windows console, WinAce, Active ACE and ACE Compression. Version 2 of ACE introduced new compression schemes that are not supported by all ACE compression software.
fmt/2076.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: ACE Archive 2
sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 7; Magic Bytes: '**ACE**' followed by byte sequence of 0x1414

fmt/2073.name: Tilt Brush
fmt/2073.description: Tilt Brush files are outputs from the Open Brush software and Google Tilt Brush application (now discontinued), which allows the user to create digital paintings in virtual reality rendering a 3D image. The format has a prepended header and the format's zipped contents contain information about brush strokes, an array of models that can be imported into the sketch and a metadata.json file. This format is a container format currently identified with a binary signature.
fmt/2073.created_by: Digital Research Alliance of Canada - FRDR / Alliance de recherche numérique du Canada – DFDR

sequences.name: Tilt Brush
sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 0; Magic Bytes: 'tilT'

fmt/2082.name: 3DM 6
fmt/2082.description: The 3DM (openNURBS) format is a 3D model representation based on non-uniform rational B-Splines (NURBS). The file format is defined by an open source C++ toolkit that reads and writes openNURBS models.

sequences.name: 3DM 6
sequences.note: BOF, offset 0, maximum offset 0: BOF: '3D Geometry File Format       6'

fmt/2074.name: MRtrix File Format
fmt/2074.description: MRtrix or MIF files are associated with the MRtrix software which provides a variety of tools for diffusion MRI analysis. MIF formats are capable of handling extra data in the text header section, which stores data in binary format.
fmt/2074.created_by: Digital Research Alliance of Canada - FRDR / Alliance de recherche numérique du Canada – DFDR

sequences.name: MRtrix
sequences.note: BOF; Offset:0; Magic Bytes: 'mrtrix image'

fmt/2084.name: 3DM 8
fmt/2084.description: The 3DM (openNURBS) format is a 3D model representation based on non-uniform rational B-Splines (NURBS). The file format is defined by an open source C++ toolkit that reads and writes openNURBS models.

sequences.name: 3DM 8
sequences.note: BOF: '3D Geometry File Format       8'

fmt/2070.description: An RCS file are indexed point cloud scan files used by Autodesk ReCap

fmt/2088.name: Final Cut Pro XML Interchange Format
fmt/2088.description: fcpxml is a specialised format used in Apple's Final Cut Pro which uses xml elements to describe the data exchanged between Final Cut Pro and third-party applications and tools.
fmt/2088.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: Final Cut Pro XML Interchange Format
sequences.note: Absolute from beginning of file, magic bytes: <?xml version=("|')1.0("|'){0-128}xmeml

fmt/2071.name: SuperCard Pro Image File
fmt/2071.description: SuperCard Pro Image File is a RAW flux format for the SuperCard Pro software and is used by many flux boards such as the Greaseweazle. It handles images for any type of floppy disk as well as tape drives and hard drives that are MFM, RLL or GCR encoded.
fmt/2071.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: SuperCard Image Pro
sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 0; Magic Bytes: 'SCP' and EOF; Offset: 0; Magic Bytes: 'FPCS'

sequences.name: SuperCard Image Pro
sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 0; Magic Bytes: 'SCP' and EOF; Offset: 0; Magic Bytes: 'FPCS'

fmt/2086.name: FieldWorks Language Explorer FWBackup
fmt/2086.description: FWBackup is a backup file native to FieldWorks Language Explorer (FLEx), an application for recording and analysing information relating to languages and cultures. The FWBackup file is a zip which stores the complete project including configuration, FWData XML, and any multimedia assets associated with the project.

fmt/2080.name: xdomea
fmt/2080.description: xdomea is an XML interface for the exchange of documents, cases, and records, typically used by digital record management systems in Germany.

sequences.name: xdomea v.3.1.0
sequences.note:
XML declaration <?xml version="1.0" (alternatively with single quotes), followed by up to 320 arbitrary bytes (rest of XML declaration + possible whitespace, comments etc.).
Xdomea namespace declaration xmlns:xdomea="urn:xoev-de:xdomea:schema:3.1.0" (alternatively with single quotes).

fmt/2083.name: 3DM 7

sequences.name: 3DM 7
sequences.note: BOF: '3D Geometry File Format       7'

fmt/2075.name: ACE
fmt/2075.description: ACE is a compressed archive format, originally developed by Marcel Lemke and later by e-merge GmbH. Some relevant software includes Ace (for DOS, OS/2 and Windows console, WinAce, Active ACE and ACE Compression.
fmt/2075.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 7; Magic Bytes: '**ACE**' followed by byte of either 0x0A, 0x0B, 0x0C or 0x0D and then either 0x0A, 0x0B, 0x0C, 0x0D or 0x14

fmt/2072.name: Weaving Information File (WIF)
fmt/2072.description: The Weaving Information File (WIF) format is a weaving information interchange format that is supported by most major weaving software packages. WIF allows users to share weaving designs between different weaving software programs and is human readable with a text editor. It is based on the Microsoft Windows .ini file format. WIF was collaboratively developed by a group of programmers of major software packages available to handweavers, including Patternland Weave Simulator, Fiberworks PCW, Swiftweave, WeaveIt, WeaveMake, Proweave and WeavePoint.
fmt/2072.created_by: The National Archives and Records Administration / The National Archives and Records Administration

sequences.name: Weaving Information File
sequences.note: BOF; Offset: 0; Magic Bytes '[WIF]' followed by carriage return 0x0D0A followed by 'Version=1.1'

fmt/2091.name: Kodak DCS RAW Image
fmt/2091.description: A Kodak DCS RAW image is a digital camera raw file from Kodak's Digital Camera System (DCS) series, saved in a proprietary format based on the Tagged Image File Format (TIFF). These files contain uncompressed, unprocessed image data.
fmt/2091.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: Kodak DCS RAW Image v.3
sequences.note: Absolute from beginning of file, magic bytes: II*.{12}KODAK{11}(DCS|EOSDCS)

fmt/2090.name: Kodak DCS RAW Image
fmt/2090.description: A Kodak DCS RAW image is a digital camera raw file from Kodak's Digital Camera System (DCS) series, saved in a proprietary format based on the Tagged Image File Format (TIFF). These files contain uncompressed, unprocessed image data.
fmt/2090.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: Kodak DCS RAW Image
sequences.note: Absolute from beginning of file, magic bytes: MM.*{60}This image file was created by a Kodak DCS{5}digital camera.

fmt/2089.name: FCPXML Interchange Format
fmt/2089.description: FCPXML files are XML-based files used by Apple's Final Cut Pro to describe project data, enabling users to transfer projects, projects, and metadata between Final Cut Pro and other compatible applications. They contain information about media, clips, editing decisions, and other project assets.
fmt/2089.created_by: Brigham Young University / Brigham Young University

sequences.name: FCPXML Interchange Format
sequences.note: Absolute from beginning of file, magic bytes: <?xml version=("|')1.0("|'){0-128}fcpxml
